title: DataCenterGym: A Physics-Grounded Simulator for Multi-Objective Data Center Scheduling
summary:
Modern datacenters schedule heterogeneous workloads across geo-distributed sites with diverse compute capacities, electricity prices, and thermal conditions. Compute utilization, heat generation, cooling demand, and energy consumption are tightly coupled, yet most existing schedulers abstract these effects and treat them independently.
  We present \textit{DataCenterGym}, a physics-grounded simulation environment for job scheduling in geo-distributed data centers, designed as a reusable testbed for future research. The simulator integrates compute queueing, building thermal dynamics, localized HVAC behavior, and temperature-dependent service degradation within a Gymnasium-compatible interface. We also develop a Hierarchical Model Predictive Control (H-MPC) scheduling algorithm that performs distributed job placement while explicitly accounting for thermal and power dynamics. Through experiments on nominal operation and workload sensitivity, we demonstrate how H-MPC improves scheduling performance relative to baseline schedulers.

AI.tldr: DataCenterGym is a physics-based simulation environment for geo-distributed data center job scheduling that integrates compute, thermal, and power dynamics, with a Hierarchical MPC scheduler that outperforms baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing schedulers treat compute utilization, heat generation, cooling demand, and energy consumption independently despite their tight coupling in modern heterogeneous, geo-distributed data centers with diverse conditions.
AI.method: Developed DataCenterGym - a physics-grounded simulation environment integrating compute queueing, building thermal dynamics, localized HVAC behavior, and temperature-dependent service degradation. Also created a Hierarchical Model Predictive Control (H-MPC) scheduling algorithm for distributed job placement accounting for thermal and power dynamics.
AI.result: Through experiments on nominal operation and workload sensitivity, H-MPC demonstrates improved scheduling performance relative to baseline schedulers.
AI.conclusion: DataCenterGym provides a reusable testbed for future research in data center scheduling, and the H-MPC approach effectively addresses the coupled nature of compute, thermal, and power dynamics in geo-distributed data centers.

title: BlockRaFT: A Distributed Framework for Fault-Tolerant and Scalable Blockchain Nodes
summary:
Blockchain technology enhances transparency by maintaining a distributed ledger among mutually untrusting parties. Despite its advantages, scalability and availability remain critical bottlenecks that hinder widespread adoption. The increasing complexity of blockchain nodes further necessitates robust fault tolerance and high throughput to ensure seamless operations.
  We present BlockRaFT, a crash-tolerant distributed framework designed to improve both the scalability and reliability of blockchain node operations. BlockRaFT framework utilizes RAFT consensus protocol to elect a leader within a cluster of systems. The elected leader coordinates and distributes workloads across follower nodes, thereby optimizing resource utilization and work load balancing. We analyzed the tasks performed by blockchain nodes and partition them according to their stateful and stateless characteristics. Stateless operations are centralized at the leader, while stateful operations are replicated and coordinated across the cluster to ensure consistency and fault tolerance. We evaluate whether this distributed intra-node architecture provides measurable benefits over traditional single-node execution models in terms of scalability, availability, and performance.
  Additionally, we introduce a concurrent Merkle tree optimization that decouples smart contract execution from tree updates, significantly reducing one of the significant performance overheads in blockchain systems. Our design philosophy is rooted in utilizing the well-established principles of distributed computing and customizing them for the blockchain domain rather than reinventing them.

AI.tldr: BlockRaFT is a crash-tolerant distributed framework that improves blockchain scalability and reliability using RAFT consensus, workload partitioning, and concurrent Merkle tree optimization.
AI.motivation: Blockchain faces scalability and availability bottlenecks despite its transparency advantages. Increasing node complexity requires better fault tolerance and throughput for widespread adoption.
AI.method: Uses RAFT consensus to elect a leader that coordinates workload distribution. Partitions blockchain node tasks into stateful (replicated across cluster) and stateless (centralized at leader) operations. Introduces concurrent Merkle tree optimization to decouple smart contract execution from tree updates.
AI.result: The paper evaluates whether this distributed intra-node architecture provides measurable benefits over traditional single-node models in terms of scalability, availability, and performance.
AI.conclusion: BlockRaFT applies established distributed computing principles to blockchain, offering a practical approach to improving scalability and reliability without reinventing fundamental concepts.

title: Accuracy Is Speed: Towards Long-Context-Aware Routing for Distributed LLM Serving
comment: Accepted to the 6th Workshop on Machine Learning and Systems (EuroMLSys '26) co-located with EuroSys '26
summary:
Distributed LLM serving systems optimize per-request latency and throughput. However, under long-context workloads, inference accuracy becomes more variable. When incorrect responses trigger retries, accuracy directly translates into cumulative user-visible delay that is not captured by single-shot latency metrics.
  In this work, we argue that under long-context serving, \textbf{accuracy becomes speed} through retry dynamics. We introduce \textit{Time-to-Correct-Answer (TTCA)}, a metric that measures the wall-clock time required to obtain the first correct response. Our measurement study shows that prompt characteristics such as length and language amplify accuracy variance, which inflates TTCA. We demonstrate \textit{Lightweight Accuracy-Aware Routing (LAAR)}, a capability-based routing design that reduces TTCA. Our results suggest that in long-context distributed serving, accuracy should be treated as a first-class systems objective.

AI.tldr: Distributed LLM serving systems need to consider accuracy as a speed factor in long-context workloads, where retries for incorrect responses create cumulative delays not captured by traditional latency metrics.
AI.motivation: Current distributed LLM serving systems focus on optimizing per-request latency and throughput, but under long-context workloads, inference accuracy becomes more variable. When incorrect responses trigger retries, accuracy directly translates into cumulative user-visible delay that is not captured by single-shot latency metrics.
AI.method: Introduces Time-to-Correct-Answer (TTCA) metric to measure wall-clock time required to obtain the first correct response. Proposes Lightweight Accuracy-Aware Routing (LAAR), a capability-based routing design that reduces TTCA by considering accuracy as a routing factor.
AI.result: Measurement study shows that prompt characteristics (length and language) amplify accuracy variance, which inflates TTCA. LAAR demonstrates effectiveness in reducing TTCA, suggesting that accuracy should be treated as a first-class systems objective in long-context distributed serving.
AI.conclusion: In long-context distributed LLM serving, accuracy becomes speed through retry dynamics, and should be treated as a first-class systems objective alongside traditional latency and throughput metrics.

title: cuNNQS-SCI: A Fully GPU-Accelerated Framework for High-Performance Configuration Interaction Selection withNeural Network QQantum States
summary: AI-driven methods have demonstrated considerable success in tackling the central challenge of accurately solving the Schrödinger equation for complex many-body systems. Among neural network quantum state (NNQS) approaches, the NNQS-SCI (Selected Configuration Interaction) method stands out as a state-of-the-art technique, recognized for its high accuracy and scalability. However, its application to larger systems is severely constrained by a hybrid CPU-GPU architecture. Specifically, centralized CPU-based global de-duplication creates a severe scalability barrier due to communication bottlenecks, while host-resident coupled-configuration generation induces prohibitive computational overheads. We introduce cuNNQS-SCI, a fully GPU-accelerated SCI framework designed to overcome these bottlenecks. cuNNQS-SCI first integrates a distributed, load-balanced global de-duplication algorithm to minimize redundancy and communication overhead at scale. To address compute limitations, it employs specialized, fine-grained CUDA kernels for exact coupled configuration generation. Finally, to break the single-GPU memory barrier exposed by this full acceleration, it incorporates a GPU memory-centric runtime featuring GPU-side pooling, streaming mini-batches, and overlapped offloading. This design enables much larger configuration spaces and shifts the bottleneck from host-side limitations back to on-device inference. Our evaluation demonstrates that cuNNQS-SCI fundamentally expands the scale of solvable problems. On an NVIDIA A100 cluster with 64 GPUs, cuNNQS-SCI achieves up to 2.32X end-to-end speedup over the highly-optimized NNQS-SCI baseline while preserving the same chemical accuracy. Furthermore, it demonstrates excellent distributed performance, maintaining over 90% parallel efficiency in strong scaling tests.

AI.tldr: cuNNQS-SCI is a fully GPU-accelerated neural network quantum state framework that overcomes CPU-GPU bottlenecks in NNQS-SCI, enabling larger quantum chemistry calculations with 2.32x speedup while maintaining chemical accuracy.
AI.motivation: The NNQS-SCI method has accuracy and scalability advantages but faces severe bottlenecks in hybrid CPU-GPU architectures: centralized CPU-based global de-duplication creates communication bottlenecks, and host-resident coupled-configuration generation causes prohibitive computational overheads, limiting application to larger systems.
AI.method: Three key innovations: 1) Distributed, load-balanced global de-duplication algorithm to minimize redundancy and communication overhead; 2) Specialized fine-grained CUDA kernels for exact coupled configuration generation; 3) GPU memory-centric runtime with GPU-side pooling, streaming mini-batches, and overlapped offloading to break single-GPU memory barriers.
AI.result: On an NVIDIA A100 cluster with 64 GPUs, cuNNQS-SCI achieves up to 2.32x end-to-end speedup over the highly-optimized NNQS-SCI baseline while preserving chemical accuracy. It demonstrates excellent distributed performance with over 90% parallel efficiency in strong scaling tests.
AI.conclusion: cuNNQS-SCI fundamentally expands the scale of solvable quantum chemistry problems by overcoming CPU-GPU bottlenecks, shifting the bottleneck from host-side limitations to on-device inference, and enabling much larger configuration spaces for accurate many-body Schrödinger equation solutions.

title: The Relic Condition: When Published Scholarship Becomes Material for Its Own Replacement
summary: We extracted the scholarly reasoning systems of two internationally prominent humanities and social science scholars from their published corpora alone, converted those systems into structured inference-time constraints for a large language model, and tested whether the resulting scholar-bots could perform core academic functions at expert-assessed quality. The distillation pipeline used an eight-layer extraction method and a nine-module skill architecture grounded in local, closed-corpus analysis. The scholar-bots were then deployed across doctoral supervision, peer review, lecturing and panel-style academic exchange. Expert assessment involved three senior academics producing reports and appointment-level syntheses. Across the preserved expert record, all review and supervision reports judged the outputs benchmark-attaining, appointment-level recommendations placed both bots at or above Senior Lecturer level in the Australian university system, and recovered panel scores placed Scholar A between 7.9 and 8.9/10 and Scholar B between 8.5 and 8.9/10 under multi-turn debate conditions. A research-degree-student survey showed high performance ratings across information reliability, theoretical depth and logical rigor, with pronounced ceiling effects on a 7-point scale, despite all participants already being frontier-model users. We term this the Relic condition: when publication systems make stable reasoning architectures legible, extractable and cheaply deployable, the public record of intellectual labor becomes raw material for its own functional replacement. Because the technical threshold for this transition is already crossed at modest engineering effort, we argue that the window for protective frameworks covering disclosure, consent, compensation and deployment restriction is the present, while deployment remains optional rather than infrastructural.

AI.tldr: Researchers extracted scholarly reasoning systems from humanities scholars' published works, converted them into AI constraints, and tested resulting scholar-bots on academic tasks with expert assessment showing high performance.
AI.motivation: To explore whether scholarly reasoning systems can be extracted from published corpora and deployed as AI agents capable of performing core academic functions at expert-level quality.
AI.method: Used an eight-layer extraction method and nine-module skill architecture grounded in local, closed-corpus analysis to distill scholarly reasoning systems into structured inference-time constraints for LLMs, then deployed scholar-bots across doctoral supervision, peer review, lecturing, and panel-style academic exchange.
AI.result: Expert assessment showed all review/supervision outputs benchmark-attaining, appointment recommendations placed both bots at or above Senior Lecturer level, panel scores ranged 7.9-8.9/10, and student surveys showed high performance ratings with ceiling effects despite participants being frontier-model users.
AI.conclusion: When publication systems make stable reasoning architectures legible and extractable, the public record becomes raw material for its own functional replacement (Relic condition), creating urgency for protective frameworks covering disclosure, consent, compensation and deployment restrictions.

title: Taming Asynchronous CPU-GPU Coupling for Frequency-aware Latency Estimation on Mobile Edge
summary: Precise estimation of model inference latency is crucial for time-critical mobile edge applications, enabling devices to calculate latency margins against deadlines and trade them for enhanced model performance or resource savings. However, the ubiquity of Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) renders traditional static profiling invalid in real-world deployments, as inference latency fluctuates with varying processor (CPU and GPU) frequencies. While extensive profiling across frequency combinations is theoretically possible, it is prohibitively expensive, particularly for emerging Small Language Models (SLMs), where variable context lengths explode the profiling up to days. We observe that simple analytic scaling fails to predict these fluctuations due to the complex asynchronous coupling between CPU (kernel launching) and GPU (execution). In this paper, we introduce FLAME to accurately estimate inference latency across frequency combinations. It features a novel layer-wise modeling that quantifies the overlapping parallelism and then aggregates dynamic pipeline bubbles caused by asynchronous processor interactions when extending to the full model. This bottom-up approach ensures generalizability across diverse models from DNNs to SLMs, and its precise modeling allows for profiling a sparse subset of samples, cutting DNN profiling from hours to minutes and SLM profiling from days to mere minutes, while maintaining small estimation errors across frequencies. We further showcase FLAME's utility in a deadline-aware DVFS, outperforming the state-of-the-art approach in both power efficiency and latency guarantees.

AI.tldr: FLAME: A system for accurate inference latency estimation across DVFS frequency combinations using layer-wise modeling of CPU-GPU asynchronous coupling, reducing profiling time from days to minutes for SLMs.
AI.motivation: Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) makes traditional static profiling invalid for real-world deployments, as inference latency fluctuates with varying processor frequencies. Profiling across all frequency combinations is prohibitively expensive, especially for Small Language Models with variable context lengths.
AI.method: FLAME uses novel layer-wise modeling that quantifies overlapping parallelism and aggregates dynamic pipeline bubbles caused by asynchronous CPU-GPU interactions. It employs a bottom-up approach that profiles only a sparse subset of samples, making it generalizable across diverse models from DNNs to SLMs.
AI.result: FLAME reduces DNN profiling from hours to minutes and SLM profiling from days to mere minutes while maintaining small estimation errors across frequencies. It enables deadline-aware DVFS that outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both power efficiency and latency guarantees.
AI.conclusion: FLAME provides an efficient and accurate solution for inference latency estimation across DVFS frequency combinations, addressing the limitations of traditional static profiling and enabling practical deployment of time-critical mobile edge applications.

title: Ragged Paged Attention: A High-Performance and Flexible LLM Inference Kernel for TPU
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) deployment is increasingly shifting to cost-efficient accelerators like Google's Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), prioritizing both performance and total cost of ownership (TCO). However, existing LLM inference kernels and serving systems remain largely GPU-centric, and there is no well-established approach for efficiently mapping LLM workloads onto TPU architectures--particularly under the dynamic and ragged execution patterns common in modern serving. In this paper, we present Ragged Paged Attention (RPA), a high-performance and flexible attention kernel for TPUs, implemented using Pallas and Mosaic. RPA addresses these challenges through three key techniques: (1) fine-grained tiling to enable efficient dynamic slicing over ragged memory, (2) a custom software pipeline that fuses KV cache updates with attention computation, and (3) a distribution-aware compilation strategy that generates specialized kernels for decode, prefill, and mixed workloads. Evaluated on Llama 3 8B on TPU7x, RPA achieves up to 86% memory bandwidth utilization (MBU) in decode and 73% model FLOPs utilization (MFU) in prefill. Integrated as the primary TPU backend in vLLM and SGLang, RPA provides a production-grade foundation for efficient TPU inference and offers practical insights into kernel design.

AI.tldr: Ragged Paged Attention (RPA) is a high-performance attention kernel for TPUs that enables efficient LLM inference on cost-efficient accelerators by handling dynamic and ragged execution patterns through fine-grained tiling, fused KV cache updates, and distribution-aware compilation.
AI.motivation: LLM deployment is shifting to cost-efficient accelerators like TPUs, but existing inference systems remain GPU-centric with no established approach for efficiently mapping LLM workloads onto TPU architectures, especially under dynamic and ragged execution patterns common in modern serving.
AI.method: RPA uses three key techniques: (1) fine-grained tiling for efficient dynamic slicing over ragged memory, (2) custom software pipeline that fuses KV cache updates with attention computation, and (3) distribution-aware compilation generating specialized kernels for decode, prefill, and mixed workloads.
AI.result: On Llama 3 8B on TPU7x, RPA achieves up to 86% memory bandwidth utilization (MBU) in decode and 73% model FLOPs utilization (MFU) in prefill. It's integrated as the primary TPU backend in vLLM and SGLang.
AI.conclusion: RPA provides a production-grade foundation for efficient TPU inference and offers practical insights into kernel design for TPU-based LLM serving systems.

title: CroSatFL: Energy-Efficient Federated Learning with Cross-Aggregation for Satellite Edge Computing
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures. Accepted by the IEEE International Symposium on Cluster, Cloud, and Internet Computing (CCGrid)
summary: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) mega-constellations extend the cloud-to-edge continuum into space, enabling satellite edge computing. However, Federated Learning (FL) in this environment is fundamentally energy-constrained due to dynamic inter-satellite connectivity, heterogeneous onboard computing hardware, and strict power budgets. We propose CroSatFL, a sustainable on-orbit hierarchical FL framework that reduces end-to-end energy across computation and communication while maintaining strong training performance under realistic LEO dynamics. CroSatFL keeps the ground station (GS) off the iterative loop by performing all local training and intermediate aggregations on orbit, requiring only two GS communication phases: one for initialization and one for final model collection. This sharply reduces repeated use of bandwidth-limited and energy-expensive GS links and shifts iterative exchanges to laser inter-satellite links (LISLs). CroSatFL integrates three energy-aware mechanisms: StarMask forms LISL-feasible clusters that align data volume with heterogeneous CPU/GPU capability, Skip-One mitigates transient stragglers by skipping at most one slow client per cluster to lower round energy and latency while preserving long-term fairness, and random-k cross-aggregation enables lightweight topology-aware cross-cluster mixing without extending round duration. Using an end-to-end energy model with a realistic Walker-Delta constellation, we show that CroSatFL reduces GS communication count by over two orders of magnitude and GS transmission energy by about 6x relative to GS-centric and on-orbit baselines, while achieving competitive accuracy and faster convergence.

AI.tldr: CroSatFL is a sustainable hierarchical federated learning framework for LEO satellite constellations that reduces energy consumption by keeping ground stations out of iterative training loops and using laser inter-satellite links instead.
AI.motivation: Federated learning in LEO mega-constellations faces fundamental energy constraints due to dynamic connectivity, heterogeneous hardware, and strict power budgets. Traditional approaches that rely heavily on ground station communication are energy-expensive and bandwidth-limited.
AI.method: CroSatFL performs all local training and intermediate aggregations on-orbit, requiring only two ground station communications (initialization and final collection). It uses three energy-aware mechanisms: StarMask for LISL-feasible clustering aligned with hardware capabilities, Skip-One to mitigate transient stragglers, and random-k cross-aggregation for lightweight topology-aware mixing.
AI.result: CroSatFL reduces ground station communication count by over two orders of magnitude and ground station transmission energy by about 6x compared to ground-station-centric and on-orbit baselines, while achieving competitive accuracy and faster convergence.
AI.conclusion: CroSatFL enables sustainable federated learning in LEO satellite constellations by dramatically reducing energy consumption through on-orbit hierarchical aggregation and intelligent use of laser inter-satellite links, making satellite edge computing more practical and energy-efficient.

title: When does a control system compute? Digital, mechanical and open-loop systems
summary: Control systems are ubiquitous in modern technology, comprising an engineered plant to be kept within specific, often fine-tuned, limits, and a separate controller that ensures this is the case. While modern controllers often employ digital computers, other examples are purely mechanical, or even biological. It is an open question whether computation is happening within all controllers by virtue of them being part of a control system. Abstraction/ Representation theory (ART) has been developed to tackle just this question of whether a physical system is computing. Here, we demonstrate how to use ART to model control systems, and analyse them for computational properties. We determine that the plant of a control system is (a proxy for) the representational entity necessary in ART for the existence of any computation: the plant is the user of the controller. We consider specific systems: a digital thermostat, an electro-mechanical thermostat, the purely mechanical centrifugal governor, and an open-loop human-controlled heating system. We show that all these systems, and control systems in general, are performing some degree of computation. As an initial use of these results, we apply them to computationalism within cognitive theory: we show the governor is computing, so it cannot play its role of counter-example in the question of whether the brain is too.

AI.tldr: The paper applies Abstraction/Representation Theory (ART) to analyze whether control systems perform computation, concluding that all control systems perform some degree of computation, including mechanical governors, and uses this to address computationalism in cognitive theory.
AI.motivation: To address the open question of whether computation occurs within all controllers by virtue of them being part of a control system, using ART to analyze various control systems including mechanical, digital, and biological examples.
AI.method: The authors apply Abstraction/Representation Theory (ART) to model control systems and analyze them for computational properties. They examine specific systems: digital thermostat, electro-mechanical thermostat, centrifugal governor, and open-loop human-controlled heating system.
AI.result: The analysis shows that all control systems, including purely mechanical ones like the centrifugal governor, perform some degree of computation. The plant is identified as the representational entity necessary for computation in ART, serving as the "user" of the controller.
AI.conclusion: All control systems perform computation to some degree. This finding has implications for computationalism in cognitive theory, showing that mechanical governors compute and therefore cannot serve as counter-examples to the claim that the brain computes.

title: AutoFlows++: Hierarchical Message Flow Mining for System on Chip Designs
summary: Understanding communication behavior in modern system-on-chip (SoC) designs is critical for functional verification, performance analysis, and post-silicon debugging. Communication traces capture message exchanges among system components and provide valuable insights into system behavior. However, deriving concise communication specifications from such traces remains challenging due to interleaved instances of communication flows, and ambiguous causal relationships among messages. Existing mining approaches often struggle with scalability and ambiguity when traces contain complex interleaving of message patterns across multiple components. These conditions often lead to an explosion in the number of candidate flows and inaccurate extraction of communication behaviors. This paper presents AutoFlows++, a design-architecture-guided hierarchical framework for mining message flows from communication traces of complex SoC designs. AutoFlows++ operates in two stages: local mining followed by global mining. In the local mining stage, simple communication patterns are extracted from traces observed at individual communication interfaces between components. In the global mining stage, these local patterns are composed to identify higher-level message flows that characterize communication behavior across multiple components. Experimental results on both synthetic traces and traces generated from SoC models in GEM5 demonstrate that AutoFlows++ significantly improves flow extraction accuracy compared with prior approaches, highlighting its effectiveness for practical SoC validation tasks.

AI.tldr: AutoFlows++ is a hierarchical framework for mining message flows from SoC communication traces, using local mining at interfaces followed by global composition to handle complex interleaving and improve accuracy over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Understanding communication behavior in SoC designs is crucial for verification, performance analysis, and debugging, but existing mining approaches struggle with scalability and ambiguity when traces contain complex interleaving of message patterns across multiple components.
AI.method: Two-stage hierarchical framework: 1) Local mining extracts simple communication patterns from traces at individual communication interfaces between components; 2) Global mining composes these local patterns to identify higher-level message flows across multiple components.
AI.result: Experimental results on synthetic traces and traces from SoC models in GEM5 show that AutoFlows++ significantly improves flow extraction accuracy compared with prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: AutoFlows++ is effective for practical SoC validation tasks, addressing challenges of interleaved communication flows and ambiguous causal relationships in complex SoC designs.

title: Breaking the Training Barrier of Billion-Parameter Universal Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials
summary: Universal Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (uMLIPs), pre-trained on massively diverse datasets encompassing inorganic materials and organic molecules across the entire periodic table, serve as foundational models for quantum-accurate physical simulations. However, uMLIP training requires second-order derivatives, which lack corresponding parallel training frameworks; moreover, scaling to the billion-parameter regime causes explosive growth in computation and communication overhead, making its training a tremendous challenge. We introduce MatRIS-MoE, a billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model built upon invariant architecture, and {Janus}, a pioneering high-dimensional distributed training framework for uMLIPs with hardware-aware optimizations. Deployed across two Exascale supercomputers, our code attains a peak performance of 1.2/1.0 EFLOPS (24\%/{35.5\%} of theoretical peak) in single precision at over 90\% parallel efficiency, compressing the training of billion-parameter uMLIPs from weeks to hours. This work establishes a new high-water mark for AI-for-Science (AI4S) foundation models at Exascale and provides essential infrastructure for rapid scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: MatRIS-MoE is a billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts universal ML interatomic potential model, trained with Janus distributed framework achieving exascale performance, compressing training from weeks to hours.
AI.motivation: Universal ML interatomic potentials (uMLIPs) require second-order derivatives and lack parallel training frameworks; scaling to billion parameters causes explosive computation/communication overhead, making training extremely challenging.
AI.method: Developed MatRIS-MoE (billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model with invariant architecture) and Janus (pioneering high-dimensional distributed training framework with hardware-aware optimizations) deployed across two Exascale supercomputers.
AI.result: Achieved peak performance of 1.2/1.0 EFLOPS (24%/35.5% of theoretical peak) in single precision with over 90% parallel efficiency, compressing billion-parameter uMLIP training from weeks to hours.
AI.conclusion: Establishes new benchmark for AI4S foundation models at Exascale and provides essential infrastructure for rapid scientific discovery.

title: GEN-Graph: Heterogeneous PIM Accelerator for General Computational Patterns in Graph-based Dynamic Programming
summary:
While graph-based dynamic programming (DP) is a cornerstone of genomics and network analytics, its efficiency is hampered by fundamentally conflicting computational patterns. Matrix-centric DP drives regular, compute-bound network analytics, while topology-centric DP handles irregular, memory-bound genomic traversals. These two categories of DP have substantially different computation patterns and dataflows, which makes it difficult for a single homogeneous processing-in-memory (PIM) architecture to efficiently support both.
  This work presents GEN-Graph, a novel heterogeneous PIM chiplet that integrates two types of specialized compute tiles within a 2.5D package: Matrix-tile, a processing-using-memory (PUM) tile optimized for matrix-centric workloads, such as all-pairs shortest path (APSP); and traversal-tile, a processing-near-memory (PNM) tile optimized for traversal-centric DP workloads, such as DNA sequence alignment. Our hardware-software co-design employs recursive partitioning and reconfigurable windowed bit-parallel logic to ensure exact computation. Results show the matrix tile achieves 42.8x speedup and 392x energy efficiency over the NVIDIA H100 GPU for APSP. For sequence-to-graph alignment, the traversal tile sustains 2.56 million reads/s (short-reads) and 39.3 thousand reads/s (long-reads), outperforming state-of-the-art accelerators by up to 2.56x in throughput. GEN-Graph provides the first scalable, exact solution for general DP dataflows by matching hardware specialization to algorithmic structure.

AI.tldr: GEN-Graph is a heterogeneous PIM chiplet that combines matrix and traversal tiles to efficiently handle both regular matrix-centric and irregular traversal-centric dynamic programming workloads in genomics and network analytics.
AI.motivation: Current homogeneous PIM architectures struggle to efficiently support both matrix-centric DP (regular, compute-bound) and topology-centric DP (irregular, memory-bound) due to their fundamentally different computational patterns and dataflows.
AI.method: GEN-Graph integrates two specialized compute tiles in a 2.5D package: Matrix-tile (PUM) for matrix-centric workloads like APSP, and traversal-tile (PNM) for traversal-centric DP like DNA sequence alignment, using hardware-software co-design with recursive partitioning and reconfigurable windowed bit-parallel logic.
AI.result: Matrix tile achieves 42.8x speedup and 392x energy efficiency over NVIDIA H100 GPU for APSP. Traversal tile sustains 2.56 million reads/s (short-reads) and 39.3 thousand reads/s (long-reads), outperforming state-of-the-art accelerators by up to 2.56x in throughput.
AI.conclusion: GEN-Graph provides the first scalable, exact solution for general DP dataflows by matching hardware specialization to algorithmic structure, effectively bridging the gap between matrix-centric and traversal-centric dynamic programming workloads.

title: New Kids: An Architecture and Performance Investigation of Second-Generation Serverless Platforms
summary: With the ever-increasing usage of serverless computing in both industry and academia, it is essential to understand the mechanisms that power the underlying platforms. As serverless is more than ten years old, there are different platforms with vastly different approaches. We show that, next to the traditional and popular platforms, a second generation of serverless platform has emerged. While first-generation platforms are based on containerized, centralized execution, the new generation leverages lightweight isolates and edge deployment. This evolution reduces warm request latency from approximately 40 ms to around 10 ms and reduces cold starts to an afterthought, but limits the execution environment. In this paper, we gather and analyze all publicly available information to provide detailed insights into the underlying architecture of seven platforms and then run a microbenchmark-based evaluation totaling more than 38 million function calls to gain a deeper understanding their performance.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes serverless computing platforms, identifying two generations: first-gen uses containerized centralized execution, while second-gen uses lightweight isolates and edge deployment, reducing latency and cold starts but limiting execution environments.
AI.motivation: With growing adoption of serverless computing in industry and academia, there's a need to understand the underlying platform mechanisms and their evolution, especially as different platforms have emerged with vastly different approaches over the past decade.
AI.method: The researchers gathered and analyzed all publicly available information on seven serverless platforms to understand their architectures, then conducted a microbenchmark-based evaluation totaling more than 38 million function calls to assess performance characteristics.
AI.result: The study identifies a second generation of serverless platforms that use lightweight isolates and edge deployment, reducing warm request latency from ~40ms to ~10ms and making cold starts negligible, though this comes with limitations on execution environments.
AI.conclusion: Serverless computing has evolved into two distinct generations with different architectural approaches, where second-generation platforms offer significant performance improvements but trade off execution environment flexibility.

title: Photonic AI: A Hybrid Diffractive Holographic Neural System for Passive Optical Real-Time Image Classification
comment: 18 pages, 3 figures. Operator-theoretic formulation and simulation of a hybrid diffractive-holographic optical neural system
summary: Edge intelligence is constrained by the energy and latency costs of shuttling data through electronic memory hierarchies. Optical systems offer a fundamentally different computational regime: once an input wavefront is launched into a structured medium, propagation, diffraction, and interference jointly enact a linear transformation whose cost is determined by wave physics rather than by clocked arithmetic. This paper develops a rigorous systems-level treatment of that regime and introduces a hybrid diffractive holographic architecture for image classification. The proposed model couples a Diffractive Optical Neural Network (DONN) with a Holographic Interference-Based Learning (HIBL) operator a formal map from digitally optimized phase distributions to physically realizable, fabrication-compatible interference patterns embeddable in passive optical elements. We express the full inference pipeline as a composition of encoding, phase modulation, free-space propagation, and intensity measurement operators, making explicit which quantities are learned, which are fixed by design, and where nonlinearity enters through photodetection. This operator-theoretic view resolves a persistent gap in the optical-ML literature between learning a transformation and physically realizing it. In physics-informed simulation on MNIST, a three-layer system with approximately 25,000 phase elements achieves 91.2% test accuracy with propagation-limited nanosecond-scale latency. The primary contribution is not a performance claim but a precise computational framework: learned representations can be physically embedded into structured optical media so that inference is executed by wavefront transformation through a passive, fabricated object rather than by sequential electronic multiply accumulate operations.

AI.tldr: A hybrid diffractive-holographic optical neural network architecture that performs image classification through passive wavefront transformations rather than electronic computation, achieving 91.2% accuracy on MNIST with nanosecond latency.
AI.motivation: Edge intelligence is limited by energy and latency costs of electronic memory hierarchies. Optical systems offer fundamentally different computational regime where wave physics performs linear transformations without clocked arithmetic, potentially enabling ultra-fast, low-energy inference.
AI.method: Develops a hybrid diffractive holographic architecture combining Diffractive Optical Neural Network (DONN) with Holographic Interference-Based Learning (HIBL) operator. Expresses inference pipeline as composition of encoding, phase modulation, free-space propagation, and intensity measurement operators. Maps digitally optimized phase distributions to physically realizable interference patterns embeddable in passive optical elements.
AI.result: In physics-informed simulation on MNIST, a three-layer system with approximately 25,000 phase elements achieves 91.2% test accuracy with propagation-limited nanosecond-scale latency. The framework bridges gap between learning transformations and physically realizing them in optical media.
AI.conclusion: The primary contribution is a precise computational framework showing learned representations can be physically embedded into structured optical media, enabling inference through passive wavefront transformation rather than sequential electronic multiply-accumulate operations, potentially revolutionizing edge computing.

title: Continuous benchmarking: Keeping pace with an evolving ecosystem of models and technologies
summary: Drawing on ideas from continuous integration, we present concepts of an automated benchmarking pipeline for high performance applications. Customization and collaboration have been key design goals owing to the requirements of research-software development as a continuous community effort. We have extended our previous conceptual work on systematic benchmarking workflows with the functionality of user-agnostic operations as well as continuous benchmarking. This fosters reproducibility and re-use of benchmarking results to ensure sustainable technological progress. We provide software-engineering solutions to keep pace with the rapid evolution of both large-scale models and high-performance computing systems with a view towards the scientific domains of neuroscience and artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: Automated benchmarking pipeline for HPC applications using continuous integration concepts, focusing on customization, collaboration, and reproducibility for neuroscience and AI domains.
AI.motivation: Need for sustainable benchmarking solutions that keep pace with rapid evolution of large-scale models and HPC systems, particularly for neuroscience and AI research communities.
AI.method: Extended previous systematic benchmarking workflows with user-agnostic operations and continuous benchmarking, emphasizing customization and collaboration for research-software development.
AI.result: Developed software-engineering solutions that foster reproducibility and re-use of benchmarking results to ensure sustainable technological progress in HPC applications.
AI.conclusion: Automated benchmarking pipeline enables continuous community effort in research-software development, supporting reproducibility and sustainable progress in neuroscience and AI domains.

title: VeriCWEty: Embedding enabled Line-Level CWE Detection in Verilog
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown significant improvement in RTL code generation. Despite the advances, the generated code is often riddled with common vulnerabilities and weaknesses (CWEs) that can slip by untrained eyes. Attackers can often exploit these weaknesses to fulfill their nefarious motives. Existing RTL bug-detection techniques rely on rule-based checks, formal properties, or coarse-grained structural analysis, which either fail to capture semantic vulnerabilities or lack precise localization. In our work, we bridge this gap by proposing an embedding-based bug-detection framework that detects and classifies bugs at both module and line-level granularity. Our method achieves about 89% precision in identifying common CWEs such as CWE-1244 and CWE-1245, and 96% accuracy in detecting line-level bugs.

AI.tldr: Proposes embedding-based bug detection framework for RTL code generated by LLMs, achieving high precision in identifying common vulnerabilities at module and line-level granularity.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated RTL code often contains vulnerabilities that can be exploited, while existing bug-detection methods fail to capture semantic vulnerabilities or lack precise localization.
AI.method: Embedding-based bug-detection framework that detects and classifies bugs at both module and line-level granularity.
AI.result: Achieves about 89% precision in identifying common CWEs (CWE-1244, CWE-1245) and 96% accuracy in detecting line-level bugs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively bridges the gap in RTL bug detection by providing semantic vulnerability analysis with precise localization capabilities.

title: Fleet: Hierarchical Task-based Abstraction for Megakernels on Multi-Die GPUs
summary:
Modern GPUs adopt chiplet-based designs with multiple private cache hierarchies, but current programming models (CUDA/HIP) expose a flat execution hierarchy that cannot express chiplet-level locality or synchronization. This mismatch leads to redundant memory traffic and poor cache utilization in memory-bound workloads such as LLM inference.
  We present Fleet, a multi-level task model that maps computation to memory scopes. Fleet introduces Chiplet-tasks, a new abstraction that binds work and data to a chiplet and enables coordination through its shared L2 cache. Wavefront-level, CU-level, and device-level tasks align with existing abstractions, while Chiplet-tasks expose a previously unaddressed level of the hierarchy. Fleet is implemented as a persistent kernel runtime with per-chiplet scheduling, allowing workers within a chiplet to cooperatively execute tasks with coordinated cache reuse. On AMD Instinct MI350 with Qwen3-8B, Fleet achieves 1.3-1.5x lower decode latency than vLLM at batch sizes 1-8 through persistent kernel execution and per-chiplet scheduling. At larger batch sizes, cooperative weight tiling increases L2 hit rate (from 12% to 54% at batch size 32 and from 39% to 61% at batch size 64), reducing HBM traffic by up to 37% and delivering 1.27-1.30x speedup over a chiplet-unaware megakernel baseline.

AI.tldr: Fleet introduces chiplet-aware task programming for GPUs to improve cache utilization in memory-bound workloads like LLM inference, achieving significant speedups through per-chiplet scheduling and cooperative weight tiling.
AI.motivation: Modern GPUs use chiplet designs with private cache hierarchies, but current programming models (CUDA/HIP) expose flat execution hierarchies that cannot express chiplet-level locality or synchronization, leading to redundant memory traffic and poor cache utilization in memory-bound workloads like LLM inference.
AI.method: Fleet presents a multi-level task model that maps computation to memory scopes, introducing Chiplet-tasks as a new abstraction that binds work and data to a chiplet for coordination through shared L2 cache. It's implemented as a persistent kernel runtime with per-chiplet scheduling, allowing workers within a chiplet to cooperatively execute tasks with coordinated cache reuse and cooperative weight tiling.
AI.result: On AMD Instinct MI350 with Qwen3-8B, Fleet achieves 1.3-1.5x lower decode latency than vLLM at batch sizes 1-8. At larger batch sizes, cooperative weight tiling increases L2 hit rate from 12% to 54% at batch size 32 and from 39% to 61% at batch size 64, reducing HBM traffic by up to 37% and delivering 1.27-1.30x speedup over chiplet-unaware baselines.
AI.conclusion: Fleet's chiplet-aware task programming model effectively addresses the mismatch between modern GPU architectures and current programming models, significantly improving cache utilization and performance for memory-bound workloads like LLM inference through proper exploitation of chiplet-level locality.

title: T-RBFT: A Scalable and Efficient Byzantine Consensus Based on Trusted Execution Environment for Consortium Blockchain
summary: With the continuous expansion of blockchain application scenarios, consortium chains have raised higher performance and security requirements for consensus mechanisms. Unlike public blockchains, consortium chains typically implement an admission mechanism that restricts participation to trusted entities, ensuring that most replicas are honest and the number of faulty nodes remains small under normal circumstances. In such settings, conventional Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) protocols, which are designed for worst-case adversarial scenarios, incur excessive message exchanges and computational overhead, thereby limiting performance and scalability. To address this issue, this paper proposes T-RBFT, a two-layer consensus mechanism inspired by network sharding and enhanced by the trusted execution environment (TEE). In T-RBFT, consensus nodes are first dynamically grouped based on their runtime characteristics. Then, inter-group consensus is achieved through a TEE-assisted BFT protocol, while each group internally reaches agreement using an improved Raft-based mechanism. Experimental evaluation shows that T-RBFT reduces communication overhead and latency, and achieves higher throughput compared to existing two-layer consensus protocols, providing a scalable and communication-efficient consensus protocol for permissioned blockchain networks.

AI.tldr: T-RBFT is a two-layer consensus protocol for consortium blockchains that combines network sharding with TEE assistance to reduce communication overhead and improve performance compared to traditional BFT protocols.
AI.motivation: Consortium chains with trusted entities don't need worst-case Byzantine fault tolerance, but conventional BFT protocols still incur excessive overhead, limiting performance and scalability. A more efficient consensus mechanism is needed for permissioned networks where most nodes are honest.
AI.method: T-RBFT uses a two-layer approach: 1) Dynamic grouping of consensus nodes based on runtime characteristics, 2) Inter-group consensus via TEE-assisted BFT protocol, and 3) Intra-group consensus using improved Raft-based mechanism.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows T-RBFT reduces communication overhead and latency while achieving higher throughput compared to existing two-layer consensus protocols.
AI.conclusion: T-RBFT provides a scalable and communication-efficient consensus protocol suitable for permissioned blockchain networks with trusted entities, addressing the performance limitations of conventional BFT protocols in consortium chain settings.

title: Exploring LLM-based Verilog Code Generation with Data-Efficient Fine-Tuning and Testbench Automation
summary: Recent advances in large language models have improved code generation, but their use in hardware description languages is still limited. Moreover, training data and testbenches for these models are often scarce. This paper presents a workflow that uses multi-agent models to generate testbenches for high-quality fine-tuning data. By automating testbench creation, the fine-tuned model for the specification-to-Verilog task achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods on the refined VerilogEval v2 benchmark while using less training data. This study provides a basis for future work on LLM-based HDL generation and automated verification.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent LLMs generate testbenches to create high-quality fine-tuning data for Verilog code generation, achieving SOTA performance with less training data.
AI.motivation: Limited use of LLMs in hardware description languages (HDLs) due to scarce training data and testbenches for Verilog code generation tasks.
AI.method: Workflow using multi-agent models to automatically generate testbenches, creating high-quality fine-tuning data for specification-to-Verilog tasks.
AI.result: Fine-tuned model achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods on refined VerilogEval v2 benchmark while using less training data.
AI.conclusion: Provides foundation for future work on LLM-based HDL generation and automated verification through automated testbench creation.

title: Robust Synchronisation for Federated Learning in The Face of Correlated Device Failure
summary: Probabilistic Synchronous Parallel (PSP) is a technique in distributed learning systems to reduce synchronization bottlenecks by sampling a subset of participating nodes per round. In Federated Learning (FL), where edge devices are often unreliable due to factors including mobility, power constraints, and user activity, PSP helps improve system throughput. However, PSP has a key limitation: it assumes device behavior is static and different devices are independent. This can lead to unfair distributed synchronization, due to highly available nodes dominating training while those that are often unavailable rarely participate and so their data may be missed. If both data distribution and node availability are simultaneously correlated with the device, then both PSP and standard FL algorithms will suffer from persistent under-representation of certain classes or groups resulting in inefficient or ineffective learning of certain features. We introduce Availability-Weighted PSP (AW-PSP), an extension to PSP that addresses the issue of co-correlation of unfair sampling and data availability by dynamically adjusting node sampling probabilities using real-time availability predictions, historical behavior, and failure correlation metrics. A Markov-based availability predictor distinguishes transient \emph{vs} chronic failures, while a Distributed Hash Table (DHT) layer decentralizes metadata, including latency, freshness, and utility scores. We implement AW-PSP and trace-driven evaluation shows that it improves robustness to both independent and correlated failures, increases label coverage, and reduces fairness variance compared to standard PSP. AW-PSP thus provides an availability-aware, and fairness-conscious node sampling protocol for FL deployments that will scale to large numbers of nodes even in heterogeneous and failure-prone environments.

AI.tldr: AW-PSP extends Probabilistic Synchronous Parallel (PSP) for Federated Learning by dynamically adjusting node sampling probabilities based on availability predictions to address fairness issues from correlated data distribution and node availability.
AI.motivation: Standard PSP assumes static, independent device behavior, but in real FL deployments, device availability correlates with data distribution, causing unfair synchronization where highly available nodes dominate training while less available nodes are underrepresented, leading to biased learning.
AI.method: AW-PSP uses real-time availability predictions, historical behavior, and failure correlation metrics to adjust sampling probabilities. It employs a Markov-based availability predictor to distinguish transient vs chronic failures and a Distributed Hash Table (DHT) layer for decentralized metadata management.
AI.result: Trace-driven evaluation shows AW-PSP improves robustness to independent and correlated failures, increases label coverage, and reduces fairness variance compared to standard PSP.
AI.conclusion: AW-PSP provides an availability-aware, fairness-conscious node sampling protocol for FL that scales to large numbers of nodes in heterogeneous, failure-prone environments.

title: EasyRider: Mitigating Power Transients in Datacenter-Scale Training Workloads
summary: Large-scale AI model training workloads use thousands of GPUs operating in tightly synchronized loops. During synchronous communication, start-up, shut-down, and checkpointing, GPU power consumption can swing from peak to idle within milliseconds. These large and rapid load swings endanger grid infrastructure as they induce steep power ramp rates, voltage and frequency shifts, and reactive power transients that can damage transformers, converters, and protection equipment. To solve this problem, we introduce EasyRider, a power architecture to mitigate power fluctuations at the rack level. EasyRider uses passive components and actively-controlled auxiliary energy storage to attenuate rack power swings. A software system continually monitors the energy storage system to maximize its lifetime in the presence of frequent charge/discharge cycles. EasyRider filters rack power variations to be within grid safety requirements without requiring software modifications to AI training frameworks or wasting energy. We evaluate EasyRider on a 400VDC-rated prototype system against published workload traces and our own GPU testbed, demonstrating its effectiveness across heterogeneous power levels and workload power profiles.

AI.tldr: EasyRider is a power architecture that mitigates rapid power fluctuations in large-scale AI training clusters by using passive components and actively-controlled energy storage at the rack level, ensuring grid safety without modifying AI frameworks.
AI.motivation: Large-scale AI training workloads cause rapid GPU power swings (peak to idle in milliseconds) during communication, start-up, shut-down, and checkpointing. These power fluctuations endanger grid infrastructure by inducing steep ramp rates, voltage/frequency shifts, and reactive power transients that can damage transformers, converters, and protection equipment.
AI.method: EasyRider uses passive components and actively-controlled auxiliary energy storage at the rack level to attenuate power swings. A software system continually monitors the energy storage to maximize its lifetime despite frequent charge/discharge cycles. The system filters rack power variations to stay within grid safety requirements without requiring modifications to AI training frameworks or wasting energy.
AI.result: EasyRider was evaluated on a 400VDC-rated prototype system using published workload traces and a GPU testbed. The system demonstrated effectiveness across heterogeneous power levels and workload power profiles, successfully mitigating power fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: EasyRider provides an effective solution to mitigate dangerous power fluctuations in large-scale AI training clusters, protecting grid infrastructure while maintaining energy efficiency and requiring no changes to existing AI training software frameworks.

title: Compositional Design, Implementation, and Verification of Swarms (Technical Report)
comment: 43 pages, 15 figures, Related version accepted at ECOOP 2026 conference
summary: Swarm protocols are a recently introduced formalism for specifying, implementing, and verifying peer-to-peer systems called swarms. A swarm consists of distributed agents called machines that communicate by asynchronous event propagation. Following a local-first model, each machine can progress without requiring continuous connectivity to other machines. Existing models of swarms are not compositional, making the modular development of large and complex swarm applications as well as the reuse of code difficult. We address these issues by presenting novel theory and techniques for the compositional specification, verification, and implementation of swarms. These results enable the correct compositional reuse of pre-existing swarm protocols and machine implementations. We implement these contributions in a companion software artifact which enables the automatic integration of independently designed and verified swarm components.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces compositional methods for swarm protocols, enabling modular development and verification of peer-to-peer systems through novel theory and implementation techniques.
AI.motivation: Existing swarm models lack compositionality, making modular development of complex swarm applications difficult and hindering code reuse. The authors aim to address these limitations.
AI.method: The paper presents novel theory and techniques for compositional specification, verification, and implementation of swarms, enabling correct compositional reuse of pre-existing swarm protocols and machine implementations.
AI.result: The contributions are implemented in a companion software artifact that enables automatic integration of independently designed and verified swarm components.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the compositionality problem in swarm protocols, providing both theoretical foundations and practical implementation tools for building complex swarm applications through modular, reusable components.

title: Spec2Cov: An Agentic Framework for Code Coverage Closure of Digital Hardware Designs
summary: Hardware verification is one of the most challenging stages of the hardware design process, requiring significant time and resources to ensure a design is fully validated and production-ready. Verification teams aim to maximize design coverage while ensuring correct behavior and alignment with the specification. Coverage closure, which relies on iterative constrained-random and directed testing, is still largely manual and therefore slow and labor-intensive. Recent advances show that the code generation capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) can be integrated with external tools to build agentic workflows that autonomously perform hardware design and verification tasks. In this work, we introduce Spec2Cov, an agentic framework that automatically and iteratively generates test stimulus directly from design specifications to accelerate coverage closure. Spec2Cov coordinates interactions between an LLM and a hardware simulator, managing compilation and simulation errors, parsing coverage reports, and feeding results back to the model for refinement. We present features that improve Spec2Cov's effectiveness without additional fine-tuning and evaluate their impact. Across 26 designs of varying size and complexity, including problems from the CVDP benchmark suite, Spec2Cov demonstrates promising performance, achieving 100% coverage on simpler designs and up to 49% on more complex designs.

AI.tldr: Spec2Cov is an AI agent framework that uses LLMs to automatically generate test stimuli from hardware design specifications to accelerate coverage closure in hardware verification.
AI.motivation: Hardware verification is slow and labor-intensive, with coverage closure relying heavily on manual iterative testing. There's a need to automate this process to reduce time and resources while ensuring design correctness.
AI.method: Spec2Cov coordinates interactions between an LLM and hardware simulator, automatically generating test stimuli from design specifications, managing compilation/simulation errors, parsing coverage reports, and feeding results back to the model for iterative refinement.
AI.result: Across 26 designs of varying complexity, Spec2Cov achieved 100% coverage on simpler designs and up to 49% on more complex designs, including problems from the CVDP benchmark suite.
AI.conclusion: Spec2Cov demonstrates promising performance for automating hardware verification coverage closure using LLM-based agentic workflows, potentially reducing manual effort and accelerating the verification process.

title: Logarithmic-Time Geodesically Convex Decomposition in Programmable Matter
summary: The decomposition of complex structures into simpler substructures is a powerful technique with a wide range of applications. We study the computation of decompositions in the context of programmable matter. The amoebot model is a well-established model for programmable matter, which places $n$ tiny robots called amoebots on the triangular grid. We consider the reconfigurable circuit extension of the geometric amoebot model, which allows amoebots to interconnect via so-called circuits. Amoebots can then instantaneously transmit simple beeps to all amoebots connected by the same circuit. Using reconfigurable circuits, previous papers have described a linear-time triangulation algorithm, and a logarithmic-time decomposition algorithm into so-called tunnel regions. Both algorithms only work on a restricted class of amoebot structures. In this paper, we define a decomposition into $O(|\mathcal H|)$ simple, geodesically convex regions for arbitrary amoebot structures, and show how it can compute such a decomposition in $O(\log n)$ rounds, where $|\mathcal H|$ denotes the number of holes in the amoebot structure. As a byproduct, we also improve the global maxima algorithm of Padalkin et al. (Nat. Comput., 2024) for special cases and with that also their spanning tree algorithm to $O(\log n)$ rounds w.h.p.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an O(log n) round algorithm for decomposing arbitrary amoebot structures into O(|H|) simple, geodesically convex regions using reconfigurable circuits in the amoebot model.
AI.motivation: Previous decomposition algorithms in the amoebot model only worked on restricted classes of structures. There's a need for efficient decomposition algorithms that work on arbitrary amoebot structures with holes, enabling better computation and coordination in programmable matter systems.
AI.method: Uses the reconfigurable circuit extension of the geometric amoebot model, where amoebots can interconnect via circuits to instantaneously transmit beeps. The method computes a decomposition into simple, geodesically convex regions based on the number of holes in the structure.
AI.result: The algorithm computes the decomposition in O(log n) rounds, where n is the number of amoebots and |H| is the number of holes. As a byproduct, improves global maxima and spanning tree algorithms to O(log n) rounds with high probability for special cases.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents an efficient decomposition algorithm for arbitrary amoebot structures that overcomes limitations of previous approaches, enabling faster computation of geometric properties and improving related algorithms in programmable matter systems.

title: Overmind NSA: A Unified Neuro-Symbolic Computing Architecture with Approximate Nonlinear Activations and Preemptive Memory Bypass
summary: Neuro-symbolic AI is gaining traction in domains such as large language models, scientific discovery, and autonomous systems due to its ability to combine perception with structured reasoning. However, its deployment is often constrained by high memory demands, diverse computation patterns, and complex hardware requirements. Existing hardware platforms struggle with large on-chip memory overheads, frequent pipeline stalls, limited I/O bandwidth, and inefficient handling of nonlinear operations. To address these key computational bottlenecks, we propose Overmind, a unified neuro-symbolic architecture with cross-layer optimizations. Overmind tackles these core bottlenecks through Padé approximations for universal nonlinear functions, preemptive memory bypass that eliminates costly on-chip caches, and a complete software stack that optimizes model deployment. By reconfiguring the Padé orders for approximating nonlinear functions, we also demonstrate adaptive accuracy-performance scaling. Overmind achieves an energy efficiency of 8.1 TOPS/W and a throughput of 410 GOPS for mixed neuro-symbolic workloads with minimal model accuracy loss. Compared to existing solutions, Overmind improves performance and efficiency with significantly fewer hardware resources.

AI.tldr: Overmind is a unified neuro-symbolic architecture that addresses memory, computation, and hardware bottlenecks through Padé approximations, preemptive memory bypass, and software stack optimizations, achieving high energy efficiency and throughput.
AI.motivation: Neuro-symbolic AI faces deployment constraints due to high memory demands, diverse computation patterns, and complex hardware requirements. Existing platforms struggle with large on-chip memory overheads, pipeline stalls, limited I/O bandwidth, and inefficient handling of nonlinear operations.
AI.method: Overmind uses Padé approximations for universal nonlinear functions, preemptive memory bypass to eliminate costly on-chip caches, and a complete software stack for optimized model deployment. It also features reconfigurable Padé orders for adaptive accuracy-performance scaling.
AI.result: Overmind achieves 8.1 TOPS/W energy efficiency and 410 GOPS throughput for mixed neuro-symbolic workloads with minimal accuracy loss. It outperforms existing solutions with significantly fewer hardware resources.
AI.conclusion: Overmind successfully addresses key computational bottlenecks in neuro-symbolic AI through cross-layer optimizations, enabling efficient deployment with high performance and energy efficiency while maintaining model accuracy.

title: HYPERHEURIST: A Simulated Annealing-Based Control Framework for LLM-Driven Code Generation in Optimized Hardware Design
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 5 tables. Accepted at IJCNN 2026
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promising progress for generating Register Transfer Level (RTL) hardware designs, largely because they can rapidly propose alternative architectural realizations. However, single-shot LLM generation struggles to consistently produce designs that are both functionally correct and power-efficient. This paper proposes HYPERHEURIST, a simulated annealing-based control framework that treats LLM-generated RTL as intermediate candidates rather than final designs. The suggested system not only focuses on functionality correctness but also on Power-Performance-Area (PPA) optimization. In the first phase, RTL candidates are filtered through compilation, structural checks, and simulation to identify functionally valid designs. PPA optimization is restricted to RTL designs that have already passed compilation and simulation. Evaluated across eight RTL benchmarks, this staged approach yields more stable and repeatable optimization behavior than single-pass LLM-generated RTL.

AI.tldr: HYPERHEURIST is a simulated annealing framework that treats LLM-generated RTL as intermediate candidates, using staged validation (compilation, simulation) before PPA optimization to produce more stable and repeatable hardware designs.
AI.motivation: Single-shot LLM generation struggles to consistently produce RTL designs that are both functionally correct and power-efficient, despite LLMs showing promise for rapid architectural exploration.
AI.method: Simulated annealing-based control framework with staged approach: 1) Filter RTL candidates through compilation, structural checks, and simulation to identify functionally valid designs, 2) Apply Power-Performance-Area (PPA) optimization only to validated designs.
AI.result: Evaluated across eight RTL benchmarks, the staged approach yields more stable and repeatable optimization behavior than single-pass LLM-generated RTL.
AI.conclusion: Treating LLM-generated RTL as intermediate candidates within a simulated annealing framework with staged validation enables more reliable production of functionally correct and power-efficient hardware designs.

title: Understanding Inference-Time Token Allocation and Coverage Limits in Agentic Hardware Verification
summary: Coverage closure is the most time-consuming phase of hardware verification, and recent large language model (LLM)-based coding agents offer a promising approach to automated stimulus generation. However, prior LLM-based flows do not systematically analyze which coverage holes remain difficult to close or how inference-time computation is allocated during agentic verification. As a result, the efficiency limits and failure modes of LLM-based coverage closure remain poorly understood, particularly for large designs. We present an empirical study using a two-tier agentic framework comprising a base Codex agent and an enhanced domain-specialized LangGraph system. Our framework enables a taxonomy of coverage holes: methodology-bound ceilings (integration tied-off hardware, infeasible boundaries, dead code) and reasoning frontiers (protocol sequencing, multi-module pipeline warm-up, narrow timing conditions), exposing fundamental limits of purely LLM-driven approaches. We further instrument the system to track token usage across six categories, including system prompt, design comprehension, stimulus generation, coverage feedback, error recovery, and agentic overhead. We show that domain specialization shifts token allocation toward coverage-directed reasoning and improves efficiency. Across designs, the enhanced system achieves comparable or higher coverage (95-99%) while using 4-13x fewer tokens and converging to coverage targets 2-4x faster than a general-purpose baseline. Our results characterize the limits of LLM-based coverage closure, inform benchmark design and human escalation strategies, and guide profile-driven agent design for hardware verification.

AI.tldr: LLM-based verification agents can achieve 95-99% coverage with 4-13x fewer tokens and 2-4x faster convergence than general-purpose baselines, but face fundamental limits in certain coverage hole categories.
AI.motivation: Coverage closure is the most time-consuming phase of hardware verification, and while LLM-based coding agents offer promise for automated stimulus generation, prior approaches lack systematic analysis of difficult coverage holes and inference-time computation allocation, leaving efficiency limits and failure modes poorly understood.
AI.method: Two-tier agentic framework with base Codex agent and enhanced domain-specialized LangGraph system, instrumented to track token usage across six categories (system prompt, design comprehension, stimulus generation, coverage feedback, error recovery, agentic overhead), enabling taxonomy of coverage holes.
AI.result: Enhanced system achieves 95-99% coverage across designs while using 4-13x fewer tokens and converging 2-4x faster than general-purpose baseline; domain specialization shifts token allocation toward coverage-directed reasoning; taxonomy reveals methodology-bound ceilings (integration tied-off hardware, infeasible boundaries, dead code) and reasoning frontiers (protocol sequencing, multi-module pipeline warm-up, narrow timing conditions).
AI.conclusion: Results characterize limits of LLM-based coverage closure, inform benchmark design and human escalation strategies, and guide profile-driven agent design for hardware verification, showing domain specialization improves efficiency but fundamental limits exist for purely LLM-driven approaches.

title: CIMple: Standard-cell SRAM-based CIM with LUT-based split softmax for attention acceleration
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) such as LLaMA and DeepSeek, are built on transformer architectures, which have become a standard model for achieving state-of-the-art performance in natural language processing tasks. Recently, there has been growing interest in deploying LLMs on edge devices. Although smaller LLM models are being proposed, they often still contain billions of parameters. Since edge devices are limited in their resources this poses a significant challenge for edge deployment. Compute-in-memory (CIM) is a promising architecture that addresses this by reducing data movement through the integration of computational logic directly into memory. However, existing CIM architectures support only static Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) operations which limit their configurability in supporting nonlinear operations and various types of transformer models. This paper presents a fully digital standard-cell SRAM-based CIM architecture accelerator for self-attention, called CIMple, designed to overcome these limitations, inside transformer models. The key contributions of CIMple are: 1) A novel dual-banked CIM-based fully digital self-attention accelerator using 8-bit parallel weight feeding. 2) A look-up-table (LUT) based fixed-point implementation reducing latency with minimal accuracy degradation. 3) A performance evaluation of a 32kb CIM-based self-attention accelerator implemented in 28nm, which achieves 26.1 TOPS/W at 0.85V and 2.31 TOPS/mm$^2$ at 1.2V, both with INT8 precision.

AI.tldr: CIMple is a digital SRAM-based compute-in-memory accelerator for transformer self-attention that addresses edge deployment challenges by supporting nonlinear operations and achieving high energy efficiency.
AI.motivation: Edge deployment of large language models is challenging due to resource constraints. Existing compute-in-memory architectures only support static MAC operations, limiting configurability for nonlinear operations and various transformer models.
AI.method: Proposes CIMple: a fully digital standard-cell SRAM-based CIM architecture with dual-banked design using 8-bit parallel weight feeding, LUT-based fixed-point implementation, and optimized for self-attention operations.
AI.result: A 32kb CIM-based self-attention accelerator in 28nm achieves 26.1 TOPS/W at 0.85V and 2.31 TOPS/mm² at 1.2V with INT8 precision, demonstrating high energy efficiency and area density.
AI.conclusion: CIMple successfully overcomes limitations of existing CIM architectures by enabling efficient edge deployment of transformer models through configurable support for nonlinear operations and achieving state-of-the-art energy efficiency.

title: MemExplorer: Navigating the Heterogeneous Memory Design Space for Agentic Inference NPUs
summary:
Emerging agentic LLM workloads are driving rapidly growing demand on both memory capacity and bandwidth, with different phases of inference (e.g., prefill and decode) imposing distinct requirements. Industry is responding by composing heterogeneous accelerators into single interconnected systems, as exemplified by NVIDIA's Vera Rubin platform, where each device brings its own memory architecture.
  This heterogeneity is further compounded by a widening landscape of available memory technologies: high-density on-chip SRAM, HBM, LPDDR, GDDR, and emerging options such as high-bandwidth flash (HBF), each offering different capacity, bandwidth, and power trade-offs.
  Identifying the right memory architecture for next-generation inference accelerators requires navigating a vast and rapidly evolving design space, in which the interplay between workload characteristics, NPU design dimensions, and memory system design remains largely underexplored.
  To address this challenge, we present MemExplorer, a new memory system synthesizer for heterogeneous NPU systems. MemExplorer provides a unified abstraction for modeling diverse memory technologies across different hierarchy levels (e.g., on-chip and off-chip) and automatically determines an efficient heterogeneous memory system together with NPU design choices (e.g., matrix engine size) to balance throughput and power between prefilling and decoding devices in a multi-device NPU system.
  Experimental results show that, under the same power budget for agentic workloads, MemExplorer achieves up to 2.3x higher energy efficiency than the baseline NPU and 3.23x higher than H100 in the prefill-only setting. Under equivalent performance targets in the decode setting, it further delivers up to 1.93x and 2.72x higher power efficiency over the baseline NPU and H100, respectively.

AI.tldr: MemExplorer is a memory system synthesizer for heterogeneous NPU systems that automatically designs efficient memory architectures for LLM inference workloads, achieving significant energy and power efficiency improvements over baseline NPUs and H100.
AI.motivation: Agentic LLM workloads require growing memory capacity and bandwidth, with different inference phases (prefill/decode) having distinct requirements. Heterogeneous accelerators with diverse memory technologies create a complex design space that needs systematic exploration.
