AI.method: MemExplorer provides a unified abstraction for modeling diverse memory technologies across hierarchy levels (on-chip/off-chip) and automatically determines efficient heterogeneous memory systems together with NPU design choices to balance throughput and power in multi-device NPU systems.
AI.result: Under same power budget for agentic workloads, MemExplorer achieves up to 2.3x higher energy efficiency than baseline NPU and 3.23x higher than H100 in prefill-only setting. Under equivalent performance targets in decode setting, it delivers up to 1.93x and 2.72x higher power efficiency over baseline NPU and H100 respectively.
AI.conclusion: MemExplorer effectively addresses the challenge of designing optimal memory architectures for heterogeneous NPU systems running LLM inference workloads, demonstrating significant efficiency improvements through automated exploration of the complex design space.

title: Co-Design of CNN Accelerators for TinyML using Approximate Matrix Decomposition
summary:
The paradigm shift towards local and on-device inference under stringent resource constraints is represented by the tiny machine learning (TinyML) domain. The primary goal of \gls{tml} is to integrate intelligence into tiny, low-cost devices under strict resource, energy, and latency constraints. However, the ultra-resource-constrained nature of these devices can lead to increased inference execution time, which can be detrimental in latency critical applications. At the same time, TinyML applications are often associated with sensitive data. As such, latency optimization approaches that rely on training samples are infeasible when such data is unavailable, proprietary, or sensitive, highlighting a pressing need for optimization approaches that do not require access to the training dataset and can be applied directly to pre-trained models. Replacing costly multiplications with more hardware-efficient operations, such as shifts and additions, has been proposed as an effective method for reducing inference latency. However, post-training power-of-two (Po2) approaches are scarce and, in many cases, lead to unacceptable accuracy loss.
  In this work, we propose a framework that applies approximate matrix decomposition to a given CNN in order to optimize hardware implementations subject to strict constraints and without any need of re-training or fine-tuning steps. The genetic algorithm-driven framework explores different matrix decompositions and resulting multiplier-less CNN accelerator designs for FPGA targets. A comprehensive evaluation of different TinyML benchmarks demonstrates our framework's efficacy in generating latency-optimized implementations that satisfy strict accuracy and resource constraints, achieving an average 33% latency improvement with an average accuracy loss of 1.3% compared to typical systolic array-based FPGA accelerators.

AI.tldr: A genetic algorithm framework for optimizing CNN inference latency on FPGAs without retraining, using approximate matrix decomposition to replace multiplications with shift-add operations, achieving 33% latency improvement with 1.3% accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: TinyML devices face strict resource constraints that increase inference latency, which is critical for latency-sensitive applications. Training data is often unavailable, proprietary, or sensitive, making data-dependent optimization approaches infeasible. Existing post-training power-of-two approaches are scarce and often cause unacceptable accuracy loss.
AI.method: Proposes a genetic algorithm-driven framework that applies approximate matrix decomposition to CNNs to enable multiplier-less FPGA accelerators. The framework explores different matrix decompositions and resulting hardware designs without requiring retraining or fine-tuning, replacing costly multiplications with hardware-efficient shift and add operations.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluation on TinyML benchmarks shows the framework generates latency-optimized implementations satisfying strict accuracy and resource constraints, achieving average 33% latency improvement with only 1.3% accuracy loss compared to typical systolic array-based FPGA accelerators.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively addresses the challenge of optimizing CNN inference latency on resource-constrained TinyML devices without access to training data, providing a practical solution for deploying efficient, multiplier-less accelerators on FPGAs while maintaining acceptable accuracy.
